summary: Gruber reviews Tot, a minimalist Mac and iOS scratchpad app created by Craig Hockenberry and The Iconfactory. Tot offers exactly seven color-coded scratchpads for temporary text, positioning itself as scrap paper rather than a full notes app. He praises its design details, iCloud syncing, and bold pricing model — free on Mac, $20 one-time purchase on iOS. Gruber draws a direct lineage from the classic Mac OS Note Pad desk accessory, framing Tot as a modern take on a deliberately constrained tool.
key_points: Tot deliberately limits itself to seven scratchpads, embracing constraint as a feature rather than a limitation
The pricing model is unconventional: free on Mac, $20 on iOS — pushing back against the race-to-the-bottom in iOS utility app pricing
Tot includes a Markdown toggle that Gruber initially opposed but now considers essential, with Hockenberry building a custom Markdown parser to make it work
The app offers scriptability through a URL scheme and shell script companion, enabling power-user workflows like piping output to the first empty dot
Gruber traces Tot's lineage directly to the classic Mac OS Note Pad desk accessory, which was similarly limited to eight sheets
money_quotes: Tot is most certainly not a full-blown notes app — it's like the difference between scrap paper and a notebook.
I, of all people, was actually against the Markdown feature on the grounds that parsing Markdown is a tricky can of worms.
$20 is, no question, a lot by iOS standards, but the "standards" for iOS utility app prices are so low as to make it not worthwhile to create iOS utility apps.
The Mac app is completely free. It's a gift to the Mac community.
Just that fact that Tot has excellent Help is commendable nowadays.
The limited scope of Tot reminds me of whiteboard space — expansive, but impermanent and set in size.
Tot is a very modern take on that very old idea.
key_insight: Deliberate constraint in software design — limiting Tot to seven scratchpads — creates a tool that fills a gap no full-featured notes app can, echoing a design philosophy as old as the original Macintosh.
